title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a marine archaeologist called Leo Vance talking about his work exploring a 17th-century shipwreck. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a marine archaeologist called Leo Vance talking about his work exploring a 17th-century shipwreck. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Leo Vance describes the technical challenges of excavating a historical vessel from the seabed.
extracts.audio_script: Good afternoon. My name is Leo Vance and I specialize in what many call 'wet archaeology'. Last year, I led a team to investigate the wreck of the 'Northern Star', a merchant vessel that sank in 1680. [7]I first became interested in this specific wreck after spotting an unusual shape on a sonar map[/7] during a routine survey of the English Channel. It wasn't where the historical records said it should be, which made the find even more exciting. Working at a depth of fifty metres presents significant physical challenges. The light is dim and the currents can be treacherous. [8]I found that the buildup of silt on the wooden hull was the most difficult obstacle to overcome[/8], as any sudden movement could cloud our vision for hours. We had to use specialized vacuum hoses to gently clear the debris. One of our most significant finds wasn't gold, but something more humble. [9]The discovery of a perfectly preserved leather boot gave us a rare glimpse into the daily life of the crew[/9]. It’s these personal items that really tell the story. To preserve the wood once it’s brought to the surface, we have to act fast. [10]We use a chemical called polyethylene glycol to replace the water in the timber cells[/10], preventing the wood from shrinking or cracking as it dries. It’s a slow and expensive process. Life on the research ship was intense. [11]I was surprised to find that the constant noise of the engines was the hardest thing to live with[/11] during our six-week expedition. You never truly get a moment of silence. Despite the exhaustion, the results were worth it. [12]Seeing the reconstructed hull in the museum for the first time was a deeply emotional moment[/12] for the whole team. It felt like bringing history back to life. Looking ahead, we are using new technology to map other sites. [13]I believe that 3D photogrammetry is the future of maritime heritage preservation[/13], as it allows us to create digital twins of wrecks without touching them. As we often say on deck, [14]we are not just salvaging cargo, we are 'rescuing history' from the depths of the ocean[/14]. It's a privilege to do this work.

dialogue.speaker: leo
dialogue.text: Good afternoon. My name is Leo Vance and I specialize in what many call 'wet archaeology'. Last year, I led a team to investigate the wreck of the 'Northern Star', a merchant vessel that sank in 1680. I first became interested in this specific wreck after spotting an unusual shape on a sonar map during a routine survey of the English Channel. It wasn't where the historical records said it should be, which made the find even more exciting. Working at a depth of fifty metres presents significant physical challenges. The light is dim and the currents can be treacherous. I found that the buildup of silt on the wooden hull was the most difficult obstacle to overcome, as any sudden movement could cloud our vision for hours. We had to use specialized vacuum hoses to gently clear the debris. One of our most significant finds wasn't gold, but something more humble. The discovery of a perfectly preserved leather boot gave us a rare glimpse into the daily life of the crew. It’s these personal items that really tell the story. To preserve the wood once it’s brought to the surface, we have to act fast. We use a chemical called polyethylene glycol to replace the water in the timber cells, preventing the wood from shrinking or cracking as it dries. It’s a slow and expensive process. Life on the research ship was intense. I was surprised to find that the constant noise of the engines was the hardest thing to live with during our six-week expedition. You never truly get a moment of silence. Despite the exhaustion, the results were worth it. Seeing the reconstructed hull in the museum for the first time was a deeply emotional moment for the whole team. It felt like bringing history back to life. Looking ahead, we are using new technology to map other sites. I believe that 3D photogrammetry is the future of maritime heritage preservation, as it allows us to create digital twins of wrecks without touching them. As we often say on deck, we are not just salvaging cargo, we are 'rescuing history' from the depths of the ocean. It's a privilege to do this work.

questions.explanation: He says the buildup of silt on the hull was the most difficult obstacle.

questions.question: The team was particularly interested in a (9) that they found among the wreckage.
questions.explanation: He mentions the discovery of a perfectly preserved leather boot.

questions.question: The scientists used (10) as a way to stop the ship's wood from decaying.
questions.explanation: They use this chemical to replace water in the timber cells.

questions.question: Leo admits that the (11) on the research vessel was quite difficult to endure.
questions.explanation: He was surprised that the constant noise of the engines was so hard to live with.

questions.question: Leo felt a sense of pride when he saw the (12) on display at a museum.
questions.explanation: Seeing the reconstructed hull was a deeply emotional moment.

questions.question: Leo thinks that (13) is the most promising tool for future underwater archaeology.
questions.explanation: He believes 3D photogrammetry is the future of the field.

questions.question: The crew uses the phrase (14) to describe the ultimate goal of their mission.
questions.explanation: He says they are 'rescuing history' from the depths.
